skill.name: flux-2-klein
skill.description: Generate images with Flux 2 Klein (Black Forest Labs' distilled fast variant of Flux 2) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model.
skill.summary: Fast AI image generation with Flux 2 Klein on RunComfy
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: All 83 static analysis findings evaluated as false positives. The skill is a Markdown documentation file for a legitimate image generation service. No malicious patterns confirmed after semantic review. External commands are bash examples in markdown code blocks, URLs are legitimate RunComfy endpoints, and no cryptographic or reconnaissance code exists in the documentation.

content.value_statement: Generate images rapidly with Flux 2 Klein AI model. Sub-second latency enables live art-direction sessions and fast creative iteration that batch models cannot match.
actual_capabilities: Generate images from text prompts using Flux 2 Klein 4B or 9B model variants
Sub-second image generation with 4B variant for rapid iteration
Multi-reference style transfer with up to 4 reference images
High-quality final output with 9B model at ~25 steps
Conditional edits using reference image composition and lighting
API integration via RunComfy CLI over HTTPS
limitations: Resolution ceiling of approximately 2K (for higher resolution use Seedream 5)
Aspect ratio capped at 16:9 (extreme ratios get cropped)
Prompt length limited to ~512 tokens (longer prompts degrade quality)
Text rendering accuracy lower than GPT Image 2 (route for embedded text)

use_cases.title: Rapid concept exploration
use_cases.description: Generate multiple image concepts quickly using the 4B variant for fast iteration during creative brainstorming sessions.

use_cases.title: Brand-consistent asset generation
use_cases.description: Use multiple reference images to maintain brand style consistency across generated marketing and advertising assets.

use_cases.title: Live art-direction sessions
use_cases.description: Participate in real-time creative direction with sub-second feedback, adjusting prompts and viewing results instantly.

prompt_templates.title: Simple product shot
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a product image showing [product description] with [lighting style], [background style], professional e-commerce photography
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic product visualization with minimal guidance

prompt_templates.title: Detailed scene composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an image of [subject] in [environment], [action/pose], with [style] aesthetic, [lighting type] lighting, [camera setup]
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed scene with specific artistic direction

prompt_templates.title: Brand-consistent edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Same composition and lighting as the reference image, but [specific changes]. Keep [elements to preserve] exactly as in the reference.
prompt_templates.scenario: Editing existing reference with specific modifications

prompt_templates.title: Multi-reference style
prompt_templates.prompt: Create [subject description] in the style of reference image 1 with color palette from reference image 2 and composition from reference image 3
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex style blending with multiple references

output_examples.input: Generate a vibrant hummingbird mid-flight sipping nectar from a bright pink hibiscus flower
output_examples.output: High-quality image of a hummingbird in flight with iridescent feathers, soft bokeh tropical background, macro photography style with cinematic lighting

output_examples.input: A matte ceramic mug on a reclaimed-wood table, soft northern window light
output_examples.output: Professional product photography image of ceramic mug with natural lighting, shallow depth of field, suitable for e-commerce listing

output_examples.input: Edit the reference image: change background to mountain studio, keep bottle silhouette
output_examples.output: Image with original bottle composition preserved, now set against a professional mountain studio backdrop with consistent lighting

best_practices: Front-load subjects and actions in prompts; trail with style and lighting directives for better output alignment
Use 4-8 steps on 4B variant for rapid iteration and concepting, switch to 9B with ~25 steps for final polished outputs
Keep reference images in consistent visual register (do not mix watercolor with photorealistic styles in same request)
anti_patterns: Avoid conflicting adjectives like 'minimalist + ornate' as they cancel each other and produce unclear results
Do not exceed ~512 tokens in prompts as the model degrades and does not truncate gracefully
Avoid asking for 4K resolution or ultra-wide aspect ratios beyond 16:9 as the model caps at ~2K and crops extreme ratios

faq.question: What is the difference between 4B and 9B variants?
faq.answer: The 4B variant offers sub-second latency for rapid iteration. The 9B variant provides higher fidelity and detail at the cost of longer generation time, recommended for final polished outputs.

faq.question: Can I use reference images for style transfer?
faq.answer: Yes, you can pass up to 4 reference images simultaneously. Keep all references in a consistent visual style (e.g., all photorealistic or all watercolor) for best results.

faq.question: Why does my prompt sometimes produce unexpected results?
faq.answer: Avoid conflicting adjectives and keep prompts under ~512 tokens. Use subject-first declarative grammar: state what the subject is doing before describing style and lighting.

faq.question: How many steps should I use?
faq.answer: Use 4-8 steps on the 4B variant for fast concepting. Switch to the 9B variant with ~25 steps for refinement and final output to get better texture and detail.

faq.question: Can this model render text in images?
faq.answer: The 8B Qwen3 embedder provides decent text rendering, but GPT Image 2 is more reliable for embedded text, logos, or multilingual signage.

faq.question: Is my API token stored securely?
faq.answer: Yes, runcomfy login writes tokens to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 (owner-only access). In CI environments, use the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.
